title: Container connected to multiple networks, listening in only one of them, does not work reliably
body:
### Issue Description

I want to run a `netcat` container which is a member of two networks (`net1` and `net2`) but only listens on one of its network IPs (say, `10.89.0.2`). (This is just a minimal example intended to reproduce this issue; my eventual goal is th run a HAproxy container that exposes other services run in containers.)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run this in terminal 1:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Prepare networks (only needs to be done once to observe this issue)
sudo podman network create --subnet=10.89.0.0/24 net1
sudo podman network create --subnet=10.89.1.0/24 net2

# Run the container
sudo podman run \
    --name=container-listen-test \
    --replace --rm --init -it \
    --network=net1:ip=10.89.0.2 \
    --network=net2:ip=10.89.1.2 \
    -p 10081:9999 \
    docker.io/subfuzion/netcat \
    -vlk 10.89.0.2 9999

# Clean up after ourselves
sudo podman network rm net1
sudo podman network rm net2
```

2. Run this in terminal 2: 

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
nc -z 127.0.0.1 10081
echo "Exit code: $?"
```

### Describe the results you received

In terminal 1:

```
Listening on [10.89.0.2] (family 0, port 9999)
```

(note the absence of confirmation)

In terminal 2:

```
Exit code: 1
```

This happens with _some_ containers, but when it does happen, it _always_ happens for this particular container instance. I.e. this appears to be a setup issue, not a runtime issue.

### Describe the results you expected

In terminal 1:

```
Listening on [10.89.0.2] (family 0, port 9999)
Connection from host.containers.internal 49986 received!
```

In terminal 2:

```
Exit code: 0
```

This happens with _most_ containers. When it does work, it _always_ works for this particular container instance.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

I have tested this with varying configurations. The only difference between a working and a not-working container appears to be the network interface insertion order and the firewall rule insertion order (checked over a few dozen attempts).

I have collected the outputs of `ip a`, `podman network inspect net1 net2`, and `nft list table inet netavark` for working and nonworking containers:

<details><summary>Not working</summary>

```
# ip a in container:

1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN qlen 1000
    link/loopback 00:00:00:00:00:00 brd 00:00:00:00:00:00
    inet 127.0.0.1/8 scope host lo
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 ::1/128 scope host 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

    link/ether 1a:d9:58:d6:d6:51 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff
    inet 10.89.1.2/24 brd 10.89.1.255 scope global eth1
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
    inet6 fe80::18d9:58ff:fed6:d651/64 scope link 
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

# nft list table inet netavark:

table inet netavark {
        chain INPUT {
                type filter hook input priority filter; policy accept;
                ip saddr 10.89.1.0/24 meta l4proto { tcp, udp } th dport 53 accept
                ip saddr 10.89.0.0/24 meta l4proto { tcp, udp } th dport 53 accept

                ip saddr 10.89.0.0/24 jump nv_5d6e02e0_10_89_0_0_nm24

        }

        chain NETAVARK-ISOLATION-2 {

        }

        chain nv_70a6fb77_10_89_1_0_nm24 {
                ip daddr 10.89.1.0/24 accept
                ip daddr != 224.0.0.0/4 masquerade

        chain nv_70a6fb77_10_89_1_0_nm24_dnat {

                ip saddr 127.0.0.1 tcp dport 10081 jump NETAVARK-HOSTPORT-SETMARK

        }

        chain nv_5d6e02e0_10_89_0_0_nm24 {

}
```

<details><summary>Diff between the two</summary>

```diff
--- notworking  2025-06-14 13:48:40.009465286 +0200
```

comments.body:
That is expected, the DNAT port forward can only have a single match + redirect on the host port so it cannot only forward to one ip which implementation wise happens to be the first one (I think, might depend on the firewall driver in use)

The reason it works sometimes is cause the network setup order is not deterministic, we store it inside a map and iterating that is not deterministic. https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/12850 already tracks stable ordering so I would close this issue here

I don't think rule order is the root cause here. The problem is that I can't use `--publish` to forward a port to a _single_ container network, only to _all_ of them. But since Podman/Netavark(?) generates DNAT rules for _all_ of a container's networks, the DNAT rule insertion order determines which destination address gets chosen.

What I would love to have is a way to (selectively, automagically) create DNAT rules for one specific network, but not for another.

I don't think using `podman network create --unmanaged` and setting up the bridge myself would help here, right? Since the firewall rules only get set up when the container is created?
--opt mode=unmanaged or --internal would work since they don't create any firewall rules.
